utterances: All of which is to say you're going to be a very busy reporter covering all of this in this year and the coming year.
I'm sure I will be (laughter).
All right.
That's NPR's health policy correspondent Alison Kodjak.
Thanks, Alison.
Thanks, David.
Tomorrow is New Year's Eve, and people all around the country will raise a toast or kiss a special someone to ring in 2017.
This week, we asked you to share your very special good luck traditions with us here at MORNING EDITION.
Deborah and Jim Purdon of Maplewood, N.J., wrote us how they eat sardines on New Year's Eve.
Delicious.
Yeah.
It's a good question.
I mean, it's part of North Korean behavior.
I mean, they do these sorts of things and then try to deny them, but at the same time applaud them.
We've seen them do this with other attacks against South Korea, sinking of a naval vessel, for example.
And this idea of a joint investigation is just absurd, but it's very typical of North Korean behavior.
Do you think North Korea and the regime there has benefited from all of this no matter who is to blame for the actual hack?
Sure.
I think so in two senses.
One is that they like the attention.
They feel like they've been pushed to the margins.
And they, of course, like it when all the media in the west are talking about this.
But also in the sense that, you know, they are trying to build a portfolio of asymmetric capabilities, whether its ballistic missiles, weapons of mass destruction.
And cyber has clearly been one of these elements.
They've been working on this for quite some time.
And they're exhibiting this capability now.
President Obama said strongly that there would be some kind of response.
What would that even look like do you think since there are no multinational corporations like Sony and North Korea to hack?
Yeah.
I mean, you know, one idea that people talk about is moving toward sanctions, putting them back on the terrorism list.
I certainly think those are plausible responses.

But the other is actually to go in the opposite direction, which is not to cut North Korea off from information and information technology, but really to try to flood the country with it so that they can break the regime's hold on information that they keep from the people.
So what do you make of all this in the big picture?
Is this just yet another incremental change in the relationship between the U.S.
and North Korea?
Or is this more substantial do you think?
I think it's pretty substantial just because North Korea has threatened us before with nuclear weapons and lots of statements at a very high level.
But this is something that actually was effecting - it's affecting the American people.
I mean, it's affecting them in the sense that this is a movie they can't go see on Christmas Day.
And I think it touches us more in a way than the North Koreans have done before.
And I think for many, they understand the North Korean threat better than they did two weeks ago.
Victor Cha is a professor at Georgetown University.
He is also the author of the book ""The Impossible State: North Korea, Past And Future."" He joined us here in our Washington studios.
Victor, thanks so much for coming in.
It's my pleasure.
From NPR News, this is NEWS AND NOTES.
I'm Ed Gordon.
Today, we introduce a new feature on the program.
The first Monday of every month, we'll be joined by Bruce Gordon, head of the NAACP and National Urban League president, Marc Morial.
We'll talk about the issues in the news and the latest goings on in their organizations.
Normally, they'd be on together, but today we had to split them up.
Marc Morial joined me earlier from Newark Airport.
He's in route to Atlanta to attend the funeral of civil rights leader Coretta Scott King.
I asked the Urban League head what the loss of Mrs. King means to the movement.
Well, Mrs. King, Coretta Scott King, was an activist and a human rights warrior in her own right.

But she's also been for us and for our generation our connection to her husband and her husband's legacy, the legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King, and I think her passing sort of is another demonstration of the passing of the torch or the passing of an era.

Her death with Rosa Park's death and Constance Baker Motley's death and Shirley Chisholm's death, all with the last 12 or so months is yet another indication that many great heroes and heroines who helped to change America, who were part of the second American revolution, are now passing on.
Lest we forget C.
Delores Tucker, as well.
C.
Delores Tucker, of course.
Absolutely.

Marc, let me ask you, there are a number of people who are suggesting that with the passing of these icons, that we may no longer have the foundation needed to keep the civil rights movement alive as we know it.
I know there are those of you on the front line who are doing all you can to do so.
What do you tell those who have that concern?

First of all, the civil rights era, the era of the '50s, the '60s, the '70s, were very special times in American history and in the history of African Americans, because it was such a transformation between the way things were before with legal segregation and de fact discrimination, to the way things have evolved.
Nonetheless, as is always the case, each generation has it's new set of challenges.

For our generation, it may be that our leaders are going to be less visible and less well known, less iconoclastic, if you will, but nonetheless, the battles we face which are now very much economic, very much about wealth and money and jobs.
So I think the issue is always not what will our leaders do, but what will we do for our community.

And I would also say that as America evolves into this 21st century, it's important to recognize that the civil rights generation and leadership created a new sense of equality in America, which has also affected the battle for equality by Hispanic Americans, by women, by other groups who have in effect embraced the ideals and the notions created by Dr. King and his cohorts of the 1960s, and Coretta Scott King and the work that they've done.

Marc, one of the things that we'll be able to do with this segment is talk to you and Bruce Gordon about issues and topics that the Urban League and the NAACP want people to know about and what you're working on.
Give us a sense of what you're working on and what people need to look for on the horizon in terms of important issues.
Right now, the National Urban League is preparing for the release of it's State of Black America report, which will come out late March and early April.

And this year's report is going to focus on economic issues; it's going to focus on Katrina, and the larger impact and the larger effect of Katrina to once again wake up in this nation to a discussion about poverty and economic disparities.

What's not always as well understood is that commanders want to purge these kinds of offenders especially, because they know how destructive it is to the ability to form the cohesion that you need to be successful in the battlefield.
Yes.
He's got to go global.
A Muslim that lives in Sao Paulo is as Muslim as a Muslim in Berlin or a Muslim in Cairo.

And understanding the nuances and the differences that go across the globe and talking about Muslims around the world in a manner that actually represents them, as opposed to suggesting that everybody is the same, that isn't what we're doing here.
So I'm looking at a very broad approach.
I'm focusing on the youth demographic for sure, trying to find opportunities that have not existing before.
Why the youth demographic?
Because they're the future leaders.
We do not want to get to know Muslims in a time of crisis.
We want to get to know Muslims in the time of non-crisis.
And as you build those relationships over decades, there will be an opportunity for trust and dialogue that exists.
And so it's very important that we look at this demographic.
You worked in the Bush administration.
I did.
Which engaged a number of high profile people to reach out to the world in general, and the Muslim world in particular.

President Bush himself was criticized for some of his statements, but also made a number of very definite statements that American wars abroad were not wars against Islam, for example, stood up in public appearances after 9/11 with Muslim clerics.
Some kind of effort was made to no measurable result.
What was missing in that effort?
You know, it's not my role to comment on the last administration.
We're taking a look at lessons learned in terms of communicating with, again, the younger generation.

The president, going out of the gate, the tone and the way in which he talked about the dignity of Islam, the fact that he respected Muslims as being part of the fabric of America, what the secretary has said over and over again about building things for the common good, all of these things have already shifted the tone.
And we're not even a year out.
I respect your desire not to criticize the past administration, but you must have some clear sense, I suppose, of where you're starting from and what has worked and not worked in the past.
What�
But I think it's important to understand that it's apples and oranges.
We're in a completely different frame.
The framework has changed.
The tenor of what people are telling me, on the planet, has changed.
You don't get any questions, for example, hypothetically, why is the United States such a blind supporter of Israel?

I get a lot of - you know, listen, I - for the last two years, I worked in the Europe bureau, and my mandate was to work with Muslim communities across Western Europe and talk to a wide range of folks in a time when there was a lot of angst out there and a lot of anger.
And I've gotten a lot of questions, including personal questions, about why an American Muslim would want to be involved and taking part in the U.S.
government.
I've gotten personal questions about my own faith.
I've gotten questions about what it means to be an American Muslim.
Do people question your faith and your beliefs because they perceive the United States as, essentially, attacking Muslims in various places in the world?
They've asked questions about my growing up and where I learned about Islam.
And I happily talk about the fact that I learned about Islam in America, going to a mosque in Quincy, Massachusetts.
And they don't know that there are mosques in every state in America.
They often don't know that Islam is very diverse in the United States.
They ask questions about - can you pray freely?
So when they ask me questions, it's not to sort of question how Muslim are you.
It's more to say: Can you be a Muslim in America?
And there is no other way to talk about that than to actually get into discussion and to give a chance to say that, you know, there is no conflict with being both American and being Muslim.
Is your job complicated by the fact that there are two ongoing wars in predominantly Muslim countries?
And the new administration, although it's changed policies in both places, is still fighting those wars?
There is complexity that happens because of political things that are going on.
Whether it's the war or it's, of course, Fort Hood that recently is on everybody's mind.
But the idea that any country on Earth is - especially ours - is going after a particular foreign policy based on a religion is not correct.
but it is my job to really capture this moment in time to work with communities who want to make a difference for our planet, who are interested in partnering with the United States, and others.
Let me say something that I'm sure you have heard from any number of foreign policy experts, former diplomats and others, and it was said of the Bush administration as well.
The statement that is made is that until the United States dramatically changes some of its policies in the world, there will not be a higher opinion of the United States in the Muslim world.
Do you believe that?
I believe that the next generation is engaging in ways it has never engage - they have never engaged before.
Will there still be difficulty out there because of foreign policy issues?
Of course there will be.
But this is not an effort to change quickly how popular we are.
This is truly to build partnerships that did not exist before for a long-term win at the end.
Farah Pandith, thanks very much.
